is_p_falciparum.evidence: The study explicitly focuses on Plasmodium falciparum infections. From the abstract: 'A total of 903 P. falciparum infections were sequenced'. All 903 participants had 'microscopy-confirmed P. falciparum infections' (Section 2.1).

reports_target_markers.evidence: The study reports k13 mutations (R622I at 44.3% prevalence, C580Y at 0.4%), which are both on the curated target list (Pfk13: 622, 580). Multiple other target markers are also reported: pfmdr1 (N86, Y184F at 96.8%), pfcrt (K76T, N75E, N326S, CVIET haplotype at 98.1%), pfdhfr (N51I, C59R, S108N), and pfdhps (A437G, K540E, A581G) - all curated target positions.

reports_extractable_frequencies.evidence: The paper provides numerator/denominator counts and percentages for all target markers. For example: 'The validated k13 R622I mutation was found in 369/833 samples (44.3%, 95% CI 40.9-47.7%)' and detailed breakdowns by district and kebele. pfcrt mutations: '98.1% of samples' with specific mutations (A220S 81.4%, N326S 73.3%, R371I 92.5%). pfdhfr: 'N51I and S108N mutations were detected in 99.4% and 99.2% of samples'. All frequencies are quantifiable.

sub_country_location.evidence: Data are tied to specific sub-national locations throughout. The study encompasses 26 Kebeles (the smallest administrative units in Ethiopia) in two districts: Gondar Zuria and Tach Armachiho (Section 2.1, Figure 1A). Frequencies are reported per Kebele (Figure 1C, S3 Table). The paper states: 'samples were collected from...26 Kebeles' and provides breakdowns like 'The mutation was identified in all but one Kebele (Fig 1C and S3 Table)' demonstrating fine resolution.

temporal_resolution_within_3y.evidence: Data collection spanned November 1, 2022 to October 31, 2023—a one-year period (well within 3 years). The study provides monthly resolution: 'We next examined the temporal dynamics of the k13 R622I mutation over the study period (November 1, 2022–October 31, 2023)' with monthly prevalence reported (Figure 5A, S4-S6 Tables). All extractable frequencies are binned by month or within the 1-year span.

african_field_samples.evidence: The study used field samples from clinical malaria patients in Ethiopia. Section 4.3 states: 'Microscopically confirmed P. falciparum infected patients, ≥2 years of age & signed informed written consent were included in the study' from health centers in Gondar Zuria and Tach Armachiho districts in the Amhara region of northwest Ethiopia. These are routine clinical samples from African field settings, not imported cases or lab strains.

is_primary_study.evidence: This is a primary empirical research article published in PLOS Pathogens. It reports original data from 903 P. falciparum samples collected and sequenced over one year using MIP panels and long-read whole-genome sequencing. The paper presents novel findings on drug-resistance marker prevalence, associations, and temporal dynamics—not a review, meta-analysis, commentary, or pure modelling.

duplicate_risk.evidence: No textual evidence indicates these samples are part of a larger consortium, multi-site network, previously published cohort, or prior surveillance dataset being reanalyzed. The authors collected samples prospectively from 'a health facility-based cross-sectional study over a year' from two health centers (Maksegnit and Sanja) in two districts. While the authors are affiliated with established institutions and acknowledge funding from East Africa Consortium for Clinical Research (EACCR), the samples themselves are presented as newly collected during the study period (November 1, 2022–October 31, 2023). The sequencing data are new depositions (accession numbers SAMN48791106–SAMN48792008). No indication that these are previously published or part of a pooled/multi-country dataset.

supplement.evidence: All supplementary materials referenced in the paper (S1 through S11 Figures and S1 through S6 Tables, plus S1 Data with metadata and genotype file) are fully available. The paper states: 'All sequencing data from the current study are available under accession numbers SAMN48791106–SAMN48792008 at the Sequence Read Archive' and 'The data includes de-identified patient clinical information, a data dictionary, and drug resistance genotypes, all of which are available in the supplementary tables.' No data or site-level detail that would improve extraction is withheld.

assessment.summary: A year-long genomic surveillance study of 903 P. falciparum infections from two ecologically distinct districts in northern Ethiopia (Gondar Zuria and Tach Armachiho), spanning 26 kebeles (sub-national units) and November 2022 to October 2023 with monthly resolution. The study used MIP targeted sequencing and long-read whole-genome sequencing. Key findings include high prevalence of k13 R622I (44.3% overall, 51.5% in Gondar Zuria, 35.4% in Tach Armachiho), persistent year-round transmission, significant co-occurrence with HRP2-based RDT negativity (22% overall), and detection of k13 C580Y (0.4%) with confirmed African origin. Multiple partner drug-resistance markers are reported with extractable frequencies: pfcrt (CVIET 98.1%), pfmdr1 (N86 99.8% wild-type), pfdhfr (N51I 99.4%, S108N 99.2%), and pfdhps (A437G 88.8%, K540E 89.1%). All data meet sub-national resolution and fine temporal binning criteria.
